did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper discusses a study involving a deep learning convolutional neural network (CNN) used for dermoscopic melanoma recognition. The text explicitly mentions comparing the diagnostic performance of this CNN to 58 dermatologists, citing metrics such as sensitivity, specificity, and ROC-AUC for the AI model in the context of skin cancer diagnosis.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The provided text is a 'Reply to the letter to the editor' published in Annals of Oncology. While it discusses and defends the methodology and results of a prior original research study (Haenssle et al., 2018) regarding a deep learning CNN, this specific document is a correspondence/defense rather than a primary research article or a formal review paper. The text does not present new original experimental data but rather clarifies methods and addresses critiques of previous work.

title: Reply to the letter to the editor ‘Man against machine: diagnostic performance of a deep learning convolutional neural network for dermoscopic melanoma recognition in comparison to 58 dermatologists’ by H. A. Haenssle et al.
brief_summary: This reply to the editor addresses critiques regarding the diagnostic performance of a deep learning convolutional neural network (CNN) for dermoscopic melanoma recognition compared to 58 dermatologists. The authors clarify methodological concerns about comparing the CNN's ROC-AUC with the average ROC area of dermatologists and justify the selection of the CNN's operating point. They demonstrate that the CNN's ROC-AUC of 0.864 is superior to the estimated ROC areas of dermatologists based on their mean sensitivity and specificity. Furthermore, the authors validate the CNN's specificity at the dermatologists' mean sensitivity levels using a large validation set of 400 images. The results confirm that the CNN's specificity was significantly higher than the dermatologists' mean specificities, supporting the validity of the original study's conclusions.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focuses on 'dermoscopic melanoma recognition' using a deep learning convolutional neural network (CNN). The CNN outputs a 'melanoma probability score' to distinguish melanoma from other skin lesions. The validation set of 400 images and the test-set were used to evaluate the diagnosis of melanoma.

all_task_types.details: The study involves a deep learning convolutional neural network (CNN) for 'dermoscopic melanoma recognition'. The model outputs a 'melanoma probability score' which is used to classify lesions. Performance is evaluated using sensitivity, specificity, and ROC-AUC, which are standard metrics for binary classification tasks. The operating point was selected based on a threshold in 'melanoma probability scores' (e.g., 0.971 and 0.947) to match dermatologist sensitivity levels.

datasets_used.name: Test-set (referred to as test-set-100)
datasets_used.description: The primary test set used in the original Haenssle et al. study to evaluate the diagnostic performance of the CNN compared to 58 dermatologists. It contains dermoscopic images of skin lesions.

use.testing: Used to calculate sensitivity, specificity, and ROC-AUC for the CNN and compare them with the 58 dermatologists. Specificity was calculated at operating points corresponding to the dermatologists' mean sensitivity.

datasets_used.name: Validation-set
datasets_used.description: A large validation set of 400 images used to determine the a priori operating point (threshold) for the CNN to match the dermatologists' mean sensitivity levels, ensuring the operating point was not selected post-experiment on the test data.

use.validation: Used to fix the sensitivity at the average sensitivity of dermatologists (level-I: 86.6% and level-II: 88.9%) and calculate corresponding cut-offs in melanoma probability scores (0.971 and 0.947).
use.other: Used to rule out overestimation of model performance by establishing an a priori operating point before applying thresholds to the test set.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To compare the CNN's specificity at the dermatologists' mean sensitivity levels against the dermatologists' mean specificities in level-I and level-II of the study.
To evaluate and compare the diagnostic performance of the CNN and dermatologists.

statistical_methods_used.method: ROC area calculation (mean of sensitivity and specificity)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To estimate the ROC area for dermatologists based on a single operating point, as discussed in the critique and reply.

statistical_methods_used.method: Approximate estimation methods for ROC area with limited information
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To estimate the ROC area for dermatologists using formulas from van den Hout and Katostaras and Katostara when only one operating point is available.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: The CNN's specificity is equal to the dermatologists' mean specificity.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: The CNN's specificity is significantly higher than the dermatologists' mean specificity.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The CNN's specificity was found to be significantly higher than the dermatologists' mean specificities in level-I (71.3%, P < 0.001) and level-II (75.7%, P < 0.001), confirming the validity and conclusions of the original data.

architecture_details.specific_architecture: Not reported in the provided text (the text refers to the original Haenssle et al. 2018 study for model details)

conclusions: The CNN's ROC-AUC of 0.864 is superior to the estimated ROC areas of dermatologists, which range from 0.790 to 0.906 depending on the estimation method, with best-guess estimates of 0.848 (van den Hout) and 0.818 (Katostaras and Katostara).

When comparing diagnostic performance at the dermatologists' mean sensitivity levels (86.6% for level-I and 88.9% for level-II), the CNN's specificity was significantly higher than the dermatologists' mean specificities (71.3% and 75.7%, respectively; P < 0.001 for both).

The operating point for the CNN was determined a priori using a validation set of 400 images to rule out overestimation of performance, resulting in melanoma probability score thresholds of 0.971 and 0.947 for level-I and level-II sensitivities, respectively.
The study confirms the validity of the original data's conclusions that the deep learning convolutional neural network outperforms dermatologists in dermoscopic melanoma recognition.

Comparing the CNN's ROC-AUC with the average ROC area (mean of sensitivity and specificity) of dermatologists is not ideal but represents one option; however, direct comparison of specificity at matched sensitivity levels provides a robust comparator.
important_remarks: The original study compared the CNN's ROC-AUC (0.864) with the dermatologists' 'ROC area' (mean of sensitivity and specificity), which is a methodological limitation as these are different metrics; however, the authors argue this comparison is valid and shows the CNN outperforms dermatologists.

Critics raised concerns that the CNN's operating point (threshold) was selected post-experiment to match dermatologist sensitivity, potentially overestimating model performance; the authors clarified that thresholds (0.971 and 0.947) were determined a priori using a separate validation set of 400 images.

The CNN's specificity was significantly higher than the dermatologists' mean specificities at matched sensitivity levels (Level-I: 83.7% vs 71.3%, P < 0.001; Level-II: 82.5% vs 75.7%, P < 0.001), supporting the conclusion of superior diagnostic performance.

Approximate estimation methods for dermatologists' ROC area (van den Hout: 0.848; Katostaras and Katostara: 0.818) still show the CNN's ROC-AUC (0.864) as superior, although the range of possible estimates (0.790–0.906) includes the CNN's value.
The study relies on a small test set (100 images) and a limited number of human experts (58 dermatologists), which may affect the generalizability of the results.

The ROC curve for the CNN is described as very steep in the region of interest, suggesting that small changes in the operating point could lead to large reductions in sensitivity or specificity, indicating potential instability in performance estimates near the chosen threshold.
